text: The surgeon retracts the gallbladder using a 5mm port at the anterior axillary line, holding it at Hartmann's pouch. Dissection begins at the cystic duct, removing fibro-fatty tissue and visceral peritoneum to skeletonize the area. The posterior part of Calot's triangle is dissected, avoiding vascular structures. A window is created from anterior to posterior through Calot's triangle. The cystic artery is identified, cauterized, and cut using bipolar Maryland forceps. Dissection continues towards the gallbladder fossa, clearing fibro-fatty tissue between Calot's triangle and the liver bed, achieving the critical view of safety.

The surgeon clips the cystic duct using a titanium clip applicator, placing two clips on the patient side and one on the specimen side, before cutting the duct between the clips. The gallbladder is then carefully dissected from the gallbladder fossa, with slow and cautious movements due to its close proximity to the liver bed caused by acute calculus cholecystitis, to avoid potential bleeding.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis and removes the gallbladder specimen through the 10 mm port at the epigastrium using a gallbladder extractor. Following removal, the surgical area is washed with normal saline, and the surgeon inspects for any bleeding or bile leakage.
